nodes.label: TaskUpdateTool
nodes.summary: The **TaskUpdateTool** acts as a rigorous project manager for AI agents, allowing them to modify the state of work items within a system. It ensures that every update—whether completing a task, changing an owner, or adding dependencies—adheres to a strict **Task Lifecycle Workflow** and *validates* data structure before saving changes. Beyond simple edits, it orchestrates collaboration by automatically triggering notifications and hooks to keep the entire agent team synchronized.

children.label: Tool Definition Wrapper
children.summary:
This is the foundational structure that packages the feature as a callable tool for the AI agent. It encapsulates the tool's identity (name, description), its interface (input/output schemas), and its execution logic.
Think of it as a "Skill Cartridge" for a video game character. The game system loads this cartridge to give the character the specific ability to "Update Tasks," complete with instructions on how to use that ability and what inputs are required to activate it.

children.label: Task Lifecycle Workflow
children.summary:
Defines the valid states a task can inhabit and the rules for transitioning between them. The workflow enforces a progression from `pending` to `in_progress` to `completed`, with a separate path for `deleted`.
It acts like a manufacturing assembly line. An item starts in the queue (pending), moves to the workstation (in_progress), and finally to the shipping crate (completed). If an item is defective, it is removed entirely (deleted). You cannot simply teleport items; you must follow the process state by state.

children.label: Lazy Schema Validation
children.summary:
A pattern used to define the structure of input and output data using Zod, wrapped in a lazy evaluator. This ensures that the schema definitions—which might be computationally expensive or have circular dependencies—are only compiled when actually needed.
Imagine a secure building with a guard at the door. Instead of the guard memorizing the face of every possible visitor at the start of the day (eager loading), the guard only looks up the specific credentials for a visitor in the manual the moment that person arrives (lazy loading).

children.label: Task Dependency Management
children.summary:
Mechanisms to link tasks together based on prerequisites. This abstraction allows a task to declare that it blocks others (`addBlocks`) or is blocked by others (`addBlockedBy`), updating the underlying task graph.
Think of a construction project schedule. You cannot paint the walls (Task B) until you have built the walls (Task A). This abstraction manages those "Finish A before Start B" links, ensuring the agent doesn't try to paint thin air.

children.label: Agent Collaboration & Hooks
children.summary:
A layer of logic that handles the side effects of task updates in a multi-agent environment. This includes assigning task ownership, notifying teammates via a "mailbox," and running verification checks (nudges) when tasks are completed.
It serves as the office manager. When you finish a job, the manager updates the board, emails the next person in line ("Mailbox"), and occasionally reminds you to double-check your work ("Verification Nudge") before you clock out.
